---
format:
  revealjs:
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-visibility: hidden 
    theme: [default, title_rs.scss]
    margin: 0.05
    slide-number: true
    transition: slide
    navigation-mode: vertical
    controls: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    embed-resources: true 
mainfont: "Cambria Math"
---

```{=html}
<section class="title-slide">

  <div class="header-band">
    <div class="logo-box">
      <img src="angrau_logo.jpeg" alt="ANGRAU Logo">
    </div>
    <div class="inst-block">
      <div class="university-name">
        Acharya N.G. Ranga Agricultural University
      </div>
      <div class="college-name">
        Agricultural College, Bapatla
      </div>
      <div class="dept-name">
        Department of Statistics and Computer Applications
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="mid-content">
    <div class="course-label">
      STAT 601 Advanced Data Analytics (1+2)
    </div>
    <div class="main-title">
      Robust Statistics
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="footer-band">
    <div class="presenter-block">
      <span class="role-label">Presenter</span>
      <span class="person-name">Krupesh S (BAD/2025-25)</span>
      <span class="person-detail">I Ph.D.(Ag.) Agricultural Statistics</span>
    </div>
    <div class="incharge-block">
      <span class="role-label">Course In-charge</span>
      <span class="person-name">Dr. B. Jyostna</span>
      <span class="person-detail">Teaching Associate (Statistics)</span>
    </div>
  </div>

</section>
```
# INTRODUCTION
## Departure from Normality
A contaminated normal distribution given by Tukey and Huber can be represented as,
$$F=(1-\epsilon) G+\epsilon H$$
where, $G = N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ and H is any distribution.

# M-ESTIMATORS OF LOCATION
## Location Model{.scrollable}
$x_i = \mu + u_i$
where $u_i$ $(i=1, \dots, n)$ are random variables.
$$
\begin{align*}
    F(x) &= P(x_i \leq x)        && \text{Definition of CDF} & \\
         &= P(\mu + u_i \leq x)  && \text{Substitute model } x_i = \mu + u_i & \\
         &= P(u_i \leq x - \mu)  && \text{Rearrange to isolate } u_i & \\
         &= F_0(x - \mu)         && \text{Apply error distribution } F_0 & \\
    \therefore F(x) &= F_0(x - \mu) && &
\end{align*}
$$

Here, $x_i$ are i.i.d. random variables.

Also, $F_0(x) = 1 - F_0(-x)$.

If $F_0 = N(0, \sigma^2)$, then,

- $\text{mean}(x) \sim N(0,\, \sigma^2/n)$
- $\text{med}(x) \approx N(0,\, 1.57 \times \sigma^2/n)$

Thus, for a pure normal distribution, mean is 57% more efficient than the median.

## Formulation{.scrollable}
Consider $f_0 = F_0'$, the density of the location model. Then, the joint density
of the model is given as,

$$L(x_i;\, \mu) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} f_0(x_i - \mu)$$

The MLE of $\mu$ is $\hat{\mu}$, such that,

$$\hat{\mu} = \hat{\mu}(x_i) = \arg\max_{\mu}\, L(x_i;\, \mu)$$

If $F_0$ is exactly known, the MLE will be the optimal. Since $F_0$ is only
approximately known, the goal is to find "nearly optimal" estimators for,

- when $F_0$ is normal
- when $F_0$ is approximately normal

## Estimation{.scrollable}
If $f_0$ is everywhere positive, since the logarithm is an increasing function,
$\hat{\mu}$ can be written as,

$$\hat{\mu} = \arg\min_{\mu} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \rho(x_i - \mu)$$

where,

$$\rho = -\log f_0$$

For some unknown $f_0$ and a function $\rho$, this problem can be solved using
the implicit equation,

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \psi(x_i - \hat{\mu}) = 0$$

where,

$$\psi = \rho'$$

## The Weighted Mean Interpretation{.scrollable}
The equation,

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \psi(x_i - \hat{\mu}) = 0$$

can be written as,

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} W(x_i - \hat{\mu})(x_i - \hat{\mu}) = 0$$

$$\implies \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i x_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \hat{\mu}$$

$$\implies \hat{\mu} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i}$$

::: {.callout-tip}
## Weighted Mean of Sample
$\hat{\mu} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i}$ is simply a **weighted mean** of the sample.
:::

where,

$$W(x) = \begin{cases} \psi(x)/x & \text{if } x \neq 0 \\ \psi'(0) & \text{if } x = 0 \end{cases}$$

and,

$$W_i = W(x_i - \hat{\mu})$$

## Formal Definition
A $\rho$-function will denote a function $\rho$ such that:

- $\rho(x)$ is a nondecreasing function of $|x|$
- $\rho(0) = 0$
- $\rho(x)$ is increasing for $x > 0$ such that $\rho(x) < \rho(\infty)$
- if $\rho$ is bounded, it is also assumed that $\rho(\infty) = 1$.

A $\psi$-function will denote a function $\psi$ that is the derivative of a
$\rho$-function, which implies in particular that

- $\psi$ is odd and $\psi(x) \geq 0$ for $x \geq 0$.

## Yet another interpretation
Another intuitive way to interpret an M-estimator is,
$$
\hat{\mu} = \hat{\mu} + \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \psi(x_i - \hat{\mu}) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \zeta(x_i, \hat{\mu})
$$
where
$$
\zeta(x, \mu) = \mu + \psi(x - \mu)
$$
In other words, $\hat{\mu}$ may be viewed as an average of modified observations $\zeta(x_i, \hat{\mu})$, also called pseudo observations:observations in the bulk of the data remain unchanged, while those too large or too small are truncated.

## Common M-Estimator Functions{.smaller .scrollable}

| **Estimator** | **Objective Function** $\rho(x)$ | **Influence Function** $\psi(x)$ | **Weight Function** $W(x)$ |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Huber** | $\begin{cases} \frac{1}{2}x^2 & |x| \leq k \\ k|x| - \frac{1}{2}k^2 & |x| > k \end{cases}$ | $\begin{cases} x & |x| \leq k \\ k\,\text{sgn}(x) & |x| > k \end{cases}$ | $\min\left(1,\, \dfrac{k}{|x|}\right)$ |
| **Bisquare** | $\begin{cases} \frac{k^2}{6}\left(1 - \left[1-\left(\frac{x}{k}\right)^2\right]^3\right) & |x| \leq k \\ \frac{k^2}{6} & |x| > k \end{cases}$ | $\begin{cases} x\left[1-\left(\frac{x}{k}\right)^2\right]^2 & |x| \leq k \\ 0 & |x| > k \end{cases}$ | $\begin{cases} \left[1-\left(\frac{x}{k}\right)^2\right]^2 & |x| \leq k \\ 0 & |x| > k \end{cases}$ |
| **Hampel** | $\frac{1}{C}\begin{cases} \frac{1}{2}x^2 & |x| \leq a \\ \frac{1}{2}a^2 + a(|x|-a) & a < |x| \leq b \\ \frac{a}{2}\left(2b - a + (|x|-b)\left[1+\frac{r-|x|}{r-b}\right]\right) & b < |x| \leq r \\ C & r < |x| \end{cases}$ | $\begin{cases} x & |x| \leq a \\ a\,\text{sgn}(x) & a < |x| \leq b \\ a\,\text{sgn}(x)\dfrac{r-|x|}{r-b} & b < |x| \leq r \\ 0 & r < |x| \end{cases}$ | $\begin{cases} 1 & |x| \leq a \\ \frac{a}{|x|} & a < |x| \leq b \\ \frac{a}{|x|}\dfrac{r-|x|}{r-b} & b < |x| \leq r \\ 0 & r < |x| \end{cases}$ |
| **Welsh** | $\dfrac{k^2}{2}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\left(\dfrac{x}{k}\right)^2\right)\right]$ | $x\exp\left(-\left(\dfrac{x}{k}\right)^2\right)$ | $\exp\left(-\left(\dfrac{x}{k}\right)^2\right)$ |

\* Note for Hampel: $C = \frac{a}{2}(b - a + r)$.

## Behaviour of Common M-Estimators
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "100%"

library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

huber_data <- function(x, k = 1.345) {
  rho <- ifelse(abs(x) <= k, 0.5 * x^2, k * abs(x) - 0.5 * k^2)
  psi <- ifelse(abs(x) <= k, x, k * sign(x))
  w   <- ifelse(abs(x) <= k, 1, k / abs(x))
  data.frame(x = x, rho = rho, psi = psi, weight = w, Estimator = "Huber")
}

bisquare_data <- function(x, k = 4.685) {
  u <- x / k
  rho <- ifelse(abs(x) <= k, (k^2/6) * (1 - (1 - u^2)^3), k^2/6)
  psi <- ifelse(abs(x) <= k, x * (1 - u^2)^2, 0)
  w   <- ifelse(abs(x) <= k, (1 - u^2)^2, 0)
  data.frame(x = x, rho = rho, psi = psi, weight = w, Estimator = "Bisquare")
}

hampel_data <- function(x, a = 1.5, b = 3.0, r = 8.0) {
  psi <- numeric(length(x))
  w   <- numeric(length(x))
  rho <- numeric(length(x))
  for(i in seq_along(x)) {
    val <- abs(x[i]); s <- sign(x[i])
    if (val <= a) {
      psi[i] <- x[i]; w[i] <- 1
    } else if (val <= b) {
      psi[i] <- a * s; w[i] <- a / val
    } else if (val <= r) {
      psi[i] <- a * ((r - val) / (r - b)) * s
      w[i]   <- (a / val) * ((r - val) / (r - b))
    } else {
      psi[i] <- 0; w[i] <- 0
    }
  }
  rho <- cumsum(psi) * (c(0, diff(x)))
  rho <- rho - min(rho)
  data.frame(x = x, rho = rho, psi = psi, weight = w, Estimator = "Hampel")
}

welsh_data <- function(x, k = 2.11) {
  rho <- (k^2 / 2) * (1 - exp(-(x/k)^2))
  psi <- x * exp(-(x/k)^2)
  w   <- exp(-(x/k)^2)
  data.frame(x = x, rho = rho, psi = psi, weight = w, Estimator = "Welsh")
}

x_seq  <- seq(-8, 8, length.out = 400)
df_all <- rbind(huber_data(x_seq), bisquare_data(x_seq),
                hampel_data(x_seq), welsh_data(x_seq))

df_long <- df_all %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(rho, psi, weight),
               names_to = "FuncType", values_to = "Value") %>%
  mutate(FuncType = factor(FuncType,
                           levels = c("rho", "psi", "weight"),
                           labels = c("Objective Function (\u03c1)",
                                      "Influence Function (\u03c8)",
                                      "Weight Function (W)")))

my_colors <- c("Huber"    = "#D55E00",
               "Bisquare" = "#0072B2",
               "Hampel"   = "#009E73",
               "Welsh"    = "#CC79A7")

ggplot(df_long, aes(x = x, y = Value, color = Estimator, linetype = Estimator)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dotted", color = "gray40") +
  facet_wrap(~ FuncType, nrow = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_color_manual(values = my_colors) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    legend.position    = "bottom",
    legend.key.width   = unit(1.5, "cm"),
    strip.background   = element_rect(fill = "gray95"),
    strip.text         = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank()
  ) +
  labs(title    = "Comparison of Robust Estimators",
       subtitle = "Behavior of Objective, Influence, and Weight functions across outliers",
       x        = "Standardized Residual (x)",
       y        = NULL)
```

# TRIMMED AND WINSORIZED MEAN

## Definition{.scrollable}

The $\alpha$-**trimmed mean** is defined as:

$$
\bar{x}_\alpha = \frac{1}{n - 2m} \sum_{i=m+1}^{n-m} x_{(i)}
$$

The $\alpha$-**Winsorized mean** is defined as:

$$
\tilde{x}_\alpha = \frac{1}{n} \left( m x_{(m+1)} + m x_{(n-m)} + \sum_{i=m+1}^{n-m} x_{(i)} \right)
$$

where,

* $n$: The total number of observations.
* $x_{(i)}$: The order statistics of the sample, such that $x_{(1)} \leq x_{(2)} \leq \dots \leq x_{(n)}$.
* $\alpha$: The trimming proportion, where $\alpha \in [0, 1/2)$.
* $m$: The integer number of trimmed values, defined as $m = [n\alpha]$, where $[\cdot]$ stands for the integer part.

::: {.callout-note}
## Limiting Case

* **Case $\alpha \to 0$ ($m = 0$):** No data is discarded. Both estimators reduce to the standard **Sample Mean**.

* **Case $\alpha \to 0.5$ ($m \approx n/2$):** The maximum amount of data is trimmed/replaced. Both estimators converge to the **Sample Median**.
:::

# L-ESTIMATORS

## Definition{.scrollable}

A more general class of estimators, called *L-estimators*, is defined as linear combinations of order statistics:

$$
\hat{\mu} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i x_{(i)},
$$

where the $a_i$s are given constants. For $\alpha$-trimmed means,

$$
a_i = \frac{1}{n - 2m} \text{I}(m + 1 \leq i \leq n - m),
$$

and for $\alpha$-Winsorized means

$$
a_i = \frac{1}{n} \Big( (m + 1)\text{I}(i = m + 1) + (m + 1)\text{I}(i = n - m) + \text{I}(m + 1 < i < n - m) \Big) .
$$

## Equivariance

If the coefficients of an L-estimator satisfy the conditions

$$
a_i \geq 0, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i = 1, \quad a_i = a_{n-i+1},
$$

then the estimator is shift and scale equivariant, and also fulfills the natural conditions

* If $x_i \geq 0$ for all $i$, then $\hat{\mu} \geq 0$
* If $x_i = c$ for all $i$, then $\hat{\mu} = c$
* $\hat{\mu}(-x) = -\hat{\mu}(x)$.

# M-ESTIMATORS OF SCALE

## Scale Model{.scrollable}

$x_i = \sigma u_i$ where $u_i \ (i = 1,\dots,n)$ are i.i.d. random variables with density $f_0$ and $\sigma > 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(x) &= P(x_i \leq x) & &\text{Definition of CDF} \\
&= P(\sigma u_i \leq x) & &\text{Substitute model } x_i = \sigma u_i \\
&= P\left(u_i \leq \frac{x}{\sigma}\right) & &\text{Rearrange to isolate } u_i \\
&= F_0\left(\frac{x}{\sigma}\right) & &\text{Apply error distribution } F_0
\end{aligned}
$$

$\therefore F(x) = F_0\left(\frac{x}{\sigma}\right)$

Differentiating the CDF with respect to $x$ yields the density (PDF):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x) &= \frac{d}{dx} F(x) & &\text{Definition of PDF} \\
&= \frac{d}{dx} F_0\left(\frac{x}{\sigma}\right) & &\text{Substitute derived CDF} \\
&= f_0\left(\frac{x}{\sigma}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma} & &\text{Apply Chain Rule}
\end{aligned}
$$

$\therefore f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma} f_0\left(\frac{x}{\sigma}\right)$

## Estimation{.scrollable}

The MLE of $\sigma$ is found from

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \arg\max_{\sigma} \frac{1}{\sigma^n} \prod_{i=1}^{n} f_0 \left( \frac{x_i}{\sigma} \right)
$$

Taking logs and differentiating with respect to $\sigma$, yields the condition:

$$
\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \rho \left( \frac{x_i}{\sigma} \right) = 1
$$

where $\rho(t) = t\psi(t)$ and $\psi = -f'_0/f_0$. Generalizing this result, any estimator $\hat{\sigma}$ satisfying the equation

$$
\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \rho \left( \frac{x_i}{\hat{\sigma}} \right) = \delta
$$

where $\delta$ is a positive constant, is called an M-estimator of scale.

## Dispersion Estimators{.scrollable}

* **Median Absolute Deviation (MAD)**
  It measures the median distance of observations from the sample median.

  $$
  \text{MAD} = \text{median}(|x_i - \text{median}(x)|)
  $$

* **Mean Absolute Deviation (MD)**
  The arithmetic mean of the absolute differences between observations and the sample mean.

  $$
  \text{MD} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i - \bar{x}|
  $$

* **Interquartile Range (IQR)**
  The difference between the 75th and 25th percentiles.

  $$
  \text{IQR} = x_{(n-m+1)} - x_{(m)}
  $$

  where $m \approx n/4$.
  

## Multiples of Scale{.scrollable}

If $x \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then $\text{SD}(x) = \sigma$. The other dispersion estimators are constant multiples of $\sigma$:

$$
\text{MD}(x) = c_1 \sigma \qquad \text{MAD}(x) = c_2 \sigma \qquad \text{IQR}(x) = 2c_2 \sigma
$$

Where the approximate constant values are:

$$
c_1 = 2\varphi(0) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \approx 0.7979 \qquad c_2 = \Phi^{-1}(0.75) \approx 0.675
$$

To create a dispersion estimator that "measures the same thing" as the standard deviation for the normal distribution, we define the Normalized MAD (MADN):

$$
\text{MADN}(x) = \frac{\text{MAD}(x)}{0.675}
$$

Likewise, we can normalize the MD and the IQR by dividing them by $c_1$ and by $2c_2$ respectively.

## M-Dispersion Estimators

An estimator that combines the robustness of MADN and the efficiency of SD and MD is the following:

* first compute a location M-estimator $\hat{\mu}$.
* Then apply a scale M-estimator $\hat{\sigma}$ to the centered data $x_i - \hat{\mu}$.
* Here $\hat{\sigma}$ should be normalized as described. This $\hat{\sigma}$ can be called the *M-dispersion estimator*.

## The $Q_n$ alternative{.scrollable}

Unlike the SD, MD, and MAD, an estimator called the $Q_n$ estimator (Croux and Rousseeuw, 1992) does not require centering the data.

* It is based strictly on the differences between data values: $d_{ij} = |x_i - x_j|$ for $i < j$.

* Let $m = \binom{n}{2}$ be the number of differences, ordered as $d_{(1)} \leq \dots \leq d_{(m)}$.

* Define 
  $$
  h = \lfloor \frac{n}{2} \rfloor + 1, \qquad k = \binom{h}{2}.
  $$
  The estimator is defined as the $k$-th order statistic: $Q_n = d_{(k)}$.

* Since $k \approx m/4$, $Q_n$ is approximately the first quartile of the $d_{ij}$ values.

# NUMERICAL ESTIMATION

## Location M-Estimators {.scrollable}

* **Initialization:** Start with a robust dispersion estimator ($\hat{\sigma}$) and an initial location estimator ($\hat{\mu}_0$):
  $$\hat{\sigma} = \text{MADN}(\mathbf{x}), \quad \hat{\mu}_0 = \text{Med}(\mathbf{x})$$

* **Iterative Procedure** ($k = 0, 1, 2, \dots$):
  1. Compute the weights:
     $$w_{k,i} = W \left( \frac{x_i - \hat{\mu}_k}{\hat{\sigma}} \right) \quad \text{for } i = 1, \dots, n$$
  2. Update the location estimate:
     $$\hat{\mu}_{k+1} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{k,i} x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{k,i}}$$

* **Stopping Rule:** Halt when the change falls below a tolerance parameter $\varepsilon$:
  $$|\hat{\mu}_{k+1} - \hat{\mu}_k| < \varepsilon \hat{\sigma}$$

* **Convergence Properties:**
  * If $\psi$ is *increasing*: The solution is unique.
  * If $\psi$ is *redescending*: Choosing a robust $\hat{\mu}_0$ ensures convergence to a valid solution.

---

## Scale M-Estimators {.scrollable}

* **Initialization:** Start with an initial robust dispersion estimator ($\hat{\sigma}_0$):
  $$\hat{\sigma}_0 = \text{MADN}(\mathbf{x})$$

* **Iterative Procedure** ($k = 0, 1, 2, \dots$):
  1. Compute the weights:
     $$w_{k,i} = W \left( \frac{x_i}{\hat{\sigma}_k} \right) \quad \text{for } i = 1, \dots, n$$
  2. Update the scale estimate:
     $$\hat{\sigma}_{k+1} = \sqrt{ \frac{1}{n\delta} \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{k,i} x_i^2 }$$

* **Stopping Rule:** Halt when the relative change falls below a tolerance parameter $\varepsilon$:
  $$\left| \frac{\hat{\sigma}_{k+1}}{\hat{\sigma}_k} - 1 \right| < \varepsilon$$

* **Convergence Properties:**
  * The sequence $\hat{\sigma}_k$ converges to a valid solution if $W(x)$ is:
    * bounded
    * even
    * continuous
    * nonincreasing for $x > 0$

---

## Simultaneous M-estimators {.scrollable}

* **Initialization:** Compute robust starting values for both location and dispersion: $\hat{\mu}_0, \hat{\sigma}_0$

* **Iterative Procedure** ($k = 0, 1, 2, \dots$):
  1. Compute standardized residuals:
     $$r_{k,i} = \frac{x_i - \hat{\mu}_k}{\hat{\sigma}_k} \quad \text{for } i = 1, \dots, n$$
  2. Compute dual weights ($W_1$ for location, $W_2$ for scale):
     $$w_{1k,i} = W_1(r_{k,i}), \quad w_{2k,i} = W_2(r_{k,i})$$
  3. Update estimates simultaneously:
     $$\hat{\mu}_{k+1} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{1k,i} x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{1k,i}}$$
     $$\hat{\sigma}_{k+1}^2 = \frac{\hat{\sigma}_k^2}{n\delta} \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{2k,i} r_{k,i}^2$$

* **Stopping Rule:** Halt when both estimates stabilize below a tolerance parameter $\varepsilon$:
  $$|\hat{\mu}_{k+1} - \hat{\mu}_k| < \varepsilon \hat{\sigma}_k \quad \text{and} \quad \left| \frac{\hat{\sigma}_{k+1}}{\hat{\sigma}_k} - 1 \right| < \varepsilon$$
  
# MEASURES TO QUANTIFY ROBUSTNESS {.scrollable}

* **Breakdown Point ($\varepsilon_n^*$):** The smallest fraction of data contamination that can cause the estimator $T$ to take on arbitrarily large values.

  $$
  \varepsilon_n^*(T, \mathbf{x}) = \min \left\{ \frac{m}{n} : \sup_{\mathbf{x}'} \|T(\mathbf{x}')\| = \infty \right\}
  $$

  *(where $\mathbf{x}'$ is obtained by replacing $m$ original points with arbitrary values)*

* **Influence Function ($IF$):** Measures the asymptotic bias caused by an infinitesimal point mass $\delta_x$ at $x$ on the functional $T$ at distribution $F$.

  $$
  IF(x; T, F) = \lim_{\varepsilon \to 0} \frac{T((1 - \varepsilon)F + \varepsilon \delta_x) - T(F)}{\varepsilon}
  $$

  *(A robust estimator requires a bounded Influence Function)*

* **Sensitivity Curve ($SC_n$):** The finite-sample, empirical counterpart to the Influence Function. It measures the scaled effect of adding an observation $x$ to a sample of size $n$.

  $$
  SC_n(x; T) = (n + 1) [T(x_1, \dots, x_n, x) - T(x_1, \dots, x_n)]
  $$

  *(As $n \to \infty$, $SC_n \to IF$)*
  
## Breakdown Points for Estimators

```{=html}
<style>
  .gray-table {
    width: 100%;
    border-collapse: collapse;
    font-size: 0.8em;
  }
  .gray-table th {
    background-color: #555555;
    color: white;
    padding: 10px;
    border: 1px solid #333333;
  }
  .gray-table td {
    padding: 10px;
    border: 1px solid #aaaaaa;
    color: #333333;
  }
  .gray-table tr:nth-child(even) {
    background-color: #f2f2f2;
  }
  .gray-table tr:nth-child(odd) {
    background-color: #e6e6e6;
  }
</style>
<table class="gray-table">
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Category</th>
      <th>Estimator</th>
      <th>Asymptotic Breakdown Point</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Classical (Location)</strong></td>
      <td>Sample Mean</td>
      <td>0</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Classical (Scale)</strong></td>
      <td>Standard Deviation (SD)</td>
      <td>0</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Classical (Scale)</strong></td>
      <td>Mean Absolute Deviation (MD)</td>
      <td>0</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Robust (Location)</strong></td>
      <td>Sample Median</td>
      <td>0.5</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Robust (Location)</strong></td>
      <td>&alpha;-Trimmed Mean</td>
      <td>&alpha;</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Robust (Scale)</strong></td>
      <td>MAD / MADN</td>
      <td>0.5</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Robust (Scale)</strong></td>
      <td>Q&#x2099; Estimator</td>
      <td>0.5</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>
```
  
# ILLUSTRATIONS IN R

## Dataset {.scrollable}

We use the `telef` dataset from `robustbase` — the classic Belgium telephone data
(1950–1973) recording the number of telephone calls (in tens of millions) over
24 years (Rousseeuw & Leroy, 1987). The mid-years **1964–1969** contain a known
recording error (calls logged in minutes instead of numbers), producing
**6 high-leverage outliers** that severely distort classical estimates.

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(robustbase)

data("telef")

# Two variables:
#   Year  : last two digits of calendar year (50 = 1950, ..., 73 = 1973)
#   Calls : number of telephone calls (tens of millions)
cat("n =", nrow(telef), "\n")
head(telef, 10)
summary(telef)
```

## Belgium Telephone Data

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-width: 16
#| fig-height: 7
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "100%"
# ── self-contained: all data prep inside this chunk ──────────────────────────
library(robustbase)
library(ggplot2)
data("telef")
# Year column is 50–73 (last two digits); convert to full year for display
telef$FullYear <- 1900L + as.integer(telef$Year)
# Outliers: years 1964–1969 → FullYear 1964:1969
outliers_idx  <- which(telef$FullYear %in% 1964:1969)
telef$group   <- ifelse(seq_len(nrow(telef)) %in% outliers_idx,
                        "Recording error (outlier)", "Normal observation")
telef$label   <- ifelse(seq_len(nrow(telef)) %in% outliers_idx,
                        as.character(telef$FullYear), "")
ggplot(telef, aes(x = FullYear, y = Calls, color = group)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = label), vjust = -0.9, size = 3.2,
            show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Normal observation"        = "gray40",
               "Recording error (outlier)" = "black")
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1950, 1973, by = 2)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Belgium Telephone Data: telef",
    subtitle = "Years 1964\u20131969 (recording error) are high-leverage outliers",
    x        = "Year",
    y        = "Calls (tens of millions)",
    color    = NULL
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position  = "bottom",
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        axis.text.x      = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

## Location Estimators {.scrollable}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(robustbase)

data("telef")
x <- telef$Calls   # telephone calls — contains high-leverage outliers

# ----- Huber M-estimator of Location -----
# huberM(x, k, tol, mu, s, se)
#   x  : numeric vector
#   k  : tuning constant — boundary between quadratic and linear loss
#        default k = 1.5; use k = 1.345 for 95% efficiency at the Normal
#   mu : initial location estimate, default = median(x)
#   s  : initial scale estimate,    default = mad(x)
#   se : logical; compute standard error? default FALSE
huberM(x, k = 1.5)

# ----- Trimmed Mean -----
# mean(x, trim)
#   trim : fraction (0 to 0.5) of observations dropped
#          from each tail before computing the mean
mean(x, trim = 0.10)     # removes bottom 10% and top 10%

# ----- Winsorized Mean (base R) -----
# Replaces the bottom and top `trim` fraction of values
# with the nearest kept boundary value, then takes the mean
winsor_mean <- function(x, trim = 0.10) {
  x_s <- sort(x)
  m   <- floor(trim * length(x_s))
  x_s[seq_len(m)]                              <- x_s[m + 1]
  x_s[seq(length(x_s) - m + 1, length(x_s))] <- x_s[length(x_s) - m]
  mean(x_s)
}

winsor_mean(x, trim = 0.10)

# ----- Summary -----
cat(
  "Mean           :", round(mean(x),               4), "\n",
  "Huber (k = 1.5)  :", round(huberM(x, k = 1.5)$mu, 4), "\n",
  "Trimmed  (10%)   :", round(mean(x, trim = 0.10),   4), "\n",
  "Winsorized (10%) :", round(winsor_mean(x, 0.10),   4), "\n",
  sep = ""
)
```

## Scale Estimators {.scrollable}

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(robustbase)

data("telef")
x <- telef$Calls

# ----- Classical -----
sd(x)                    # Standard deviation
IQR(x)                   # Interquartile range (Q3 - Q1)

# ----- MAD and MADN -----
# mad(x, center, constant)
#   center   : central value, default = median(x)
#   constant : 1      -> raw MAD
#              1.4826 -> MADN, consistent estimator of SD at Normal (default)
mad(x, constant = 1)     # Raw MAD
mad(x)                   # MADN

# ----- Normalized IQR -----
IQR(x) / 1.349           # Divide by 1.349 to make consistent with SD at Normal

# ----- scaleTau2 -----
# scaleTau2(x, c1, c2, consistency, mu.too, na.rm)
#   c1          : tuning constant for location weights (default 4.5)
#   c2          : tuning constant for scale rho function (default 3.0)
#   consistency : divide by asymptotic limit for consistency at Normal (default TRUE)
#   mu.too      : if TRUE, returns named vector c(mu, sigma) instead of just sigma
#   na.rm       : remove NAs before computing (default FALSE)
scaleTau2(x, mu.too = TRUE)

# ----- Qn estimator (Croux & Rousseeuw, 1992) -----
# Qn(x, constant, na.rm)
#   k-th order statistic of all pairwise |x_i - x_j|; no centering needed
#   constant : consistency factor; default gives consistency at Normal
#   na.rm    : remove NAs (default FALSE)
Qn(x)

# ----- Sn estimator (Rousseeuw & Croux, 1993) -----
# Sn(x, constant, finite.corr, na.rm)
#   median_i { median_j |x_i - x_j| }; no centering needed
#   constant    : consistency factor; default gives consistency at Normal
#   finite.corr : apply finite-sample bias correction (default TRUE)
#   na.rm       : remove NAs (default FALSE)
Sn(x)
```
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
# ----- Summary -----
cat(
  "SD         :", round(sd(x),                4), "\n",
  "MAD (raw)  :", round(mad(x, constant = 1), 4), "\n",
  "MADN       :", round(mad(x),               4), "\n",
  "IQR (norm) :", round(IQR(x) / 1.349,       4), "\n",
  "scaleTau2  :", round(scaleTau2(x),          4), "\n",
  "Qn         :", round(Qn(x),                4), "\n",
  "Sn         :", round(Sn(x),                4), "\n",
  sep = ""
)
```

## Pseudo Observations via `huberize()` {.scrollable}

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(robustbase)

data("telef")
x <- telef$Calls

# Step 1: obtain robust location via huberM()
fit <- huberM(x, k = 1.5)
cat("Huber mu :", round(fit$mu, 4), "\n")
cat("Huber s  :", round(fit$s,  4), "\n\n")

# Step 2: huberize(x, M, c, k)
#   x : numeric vector of observations
#   M : location center — default = huberM(x, k = k)$mu
#   c : half-width multiplier — boundary = [M - c*Qn(x), M + c*Qn(x)]
#       observations outside this interval are pulled to the boundary
#       default c = k = 1.5
#   k : tuning constant used when computing default M via huberM()
# NOTE: scale is computed INTERNALLY as Qn(x); it is NOT a user argument
x_pseudo <- huberize(x, M = fit$mu, c = 1.5, k = 1.5)

# Step 3: identify modified (outlying) observations
modified <- which(round(x, 8) != round(x_pseudo, 8))

cat(
  "Total observations   :", length(x), "\n",
  "Unchanged (bulk)     :", length(x) - length(modified), "\n",
  "Modified (truncated) :", length(modified), "\n\n")

# Step 4: table of original vs pseudo for modified observations
data.frame(
  Year     = 1900L + as.integer(telef$Year[modified]),
  Original = round(x[modified],        4),
  Pseudo   = round(x_pseudo[modified], 4)
)
```

## Pseudo-observations comparison
```{r}
# Load ggplot2
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)

# Create a dataframe for plotting
df_plot <- data.frame(
  Year = 1900L + as.integer(telef$Year),
  Original = x,
  Huberized = x_pseudo
)

# Plot
ggplot(df_plot, aes(x = Year)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Original, color = "Original"), linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_point(aes(y = Original, color = "Original"), size = 2) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Huberized, color = "Huberized"), size = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(y = Huberized, color = "Huberized"), size = 2, shape = 17) +
  scale_color_manual(name = "Observation",
                     values = c("Original" = "red", "Huberized" = "blue")) +
  labs(title = "Belgian Telephone Calls: Original vs. Huberized",
       subtitle = "Outliers (1964-1969) are pulled to the robust boundary",
       x = "Year",
       y = "Number of Calls (tens of millions)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "top")
```
